chunks.title: Scope and Objectives
chunks.content: This course introduces principles of human factors and ergonomics, emphasizing human capabilities, limitations, and tendencies, and design to support humans in sociotechnical work systems. Topics include human information processing, physiological and biomechanical functioning, and implications for design of the workplace and jobs in that workplace. The field of human factors and ergonomics (HFE) is interdisciplinary, with applications wherever humans interact with equipment in a system context. Examples will be drawn from many domains that involve humans interacting in systems, including manufacturing, medicine, aerospace, ground transportation, and computer systems.

chunks.title: Prerequisite
chunks.content: Grade of C or better in MEEN 221; junior or senior classification

chunks.title: Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
Students will be able to...
• demonstrate an understanding of the unique factors affecting human performance in sociotechnical systems,
• apply human factors theory to the design or selection of system elements to support human performance and safety,
• apply theory to calculate the severity of hazards due to exposure to visual, auditory, vibratory, and other environmental stimuli and identify redesign solutions for mitigating the hazards,
• apply theory to determine ideal display, control, and physical layout characteristics for supporting an operator's cognitive and physical performance, and
• demonstrate applied knowledge of course concepts in identifying human-systems interaction problems with an existing technology, and in proposing redesign solutions.

chunks.title: Text
chunks.content:
Designing for People: An Introduction to Human Factors Engineering, Third Edition. (2017).
Lee, J.D., Wickens, C.D., Liu, Y., & Ng Boyle, L.
• The text is strongly recommended. The First and Second editions of "An Introduction to Human Factors Engineering" (Wickens is lead author) are acceptable substitutes.
• Course materials are hosted on Canvas.

chunks.title: Grade Determination
chunks.content:
20% Homework and Quizzes
• Assigned throughout the semester
20% Midterm Exam 1 (tentatively covering Topics 1-4)
• Friday (2/6) during class
20% Midterm Exam 2 (tentatively covering Topics 5-8)
• Friday (3/6) during class
20% Midterm Exam 3 (tentatively covering Topics 9-12)
• Wednesday (4/1) during class
20% Final Exam (tentatively covering Topics 13-16)
• Friday (5/1) 10:30 am - 12:30 pm

*Exam topic coverage may change. If so, it will be announced in class and/or via Canvas announcement.

Grades will be calculated on the basis of total points earned, not on the average of percentage grades for each entry. Grades may be curved based on class average such that the point thresholds may be lowered from the following standard:
A: 90-100% B: 80-89% C: 70-79% D: 60-69% F: 59% and lower

Homework and Quizzes:
There will be at least one homework assignment or quiz per lecture topic. These assessments will be designed to give you practice in applying principles and ideas learned in the course. Homework assignments and solutions (when appropriate) will be posted on Canvas and announced in lecture and/or via email (Canvas announcement). Due dates for each assignment will be given when they are issued but will generally be within one week. Unless otherwise specified, completed assignments should be submitted electronically via Canvas. The total homework and quizzes grade will be based on total points, not on the average of percentage grades for each entry.

Exams:
There will be four exams, each worth 20% of the overall course grade. Collaboration on exams is not allowed and will result in offending parties receiving no credit on the exam (see Academic Integrity and Misconduct Section below for further consequences). The exams will emphasize material discussed in lecture and practiced in the homework and quizzes; material exclusively in the text will not be tested but may aid in providing background information. Grades will be posted on the course Canvas website and students can review their graded exams during office hours.

Regrading Policy:
Students have one week after grades are released for a homework, quiz, or exam to submit a regrade request via email to Dr. McKenzie. This request must not exceed one page (11-point font, single spacing), and must clearly indicate the relevant problem(s) and justification for why you think regrading is warranted. Note that a requested regrade may result in further point deductions if new errors are discovered.

chunks.title: Lecture Topics (subject to change)
chunks.content:
Any substantial changes will be announced in class/via email and an updated schedule will be available on Canvas. Note: due dates for homework will be posted with each assignment.
0: Course Introduction
1: Introduction to HFE
2: Vision
3: Illumination in the workplace
4: Displays
5: Controls and Dynamics
6: Documentation and Warnings
7: Hearing and Noise
8: Supporting Cognitive Functions
9: Human Error
10: Motor Skills & Control
11: Anthropometry
12: Workspace Design
13: Work Physiology
14: Biomechanics and Manual Material Handling
15: Cumulative Trauma Disorders
16: Environmental Stressors

chunks.title: Late Policy
chunks.content:
Late work is accepted up to three days past the due date, with a penalty of 25% off per day.

Exam dates will be flexible only in case of sickness, military service, jury duty, presentation at a professional conference, or death in the family, according to university rules (https://student-rules.tamu.edu/rule07/). Documentation is required in all cases.

chunks.title: AI text generators policy
chunks.content:
AI text generators (ChatGPT, Google Bard, etc.) should not be used for any work for this class without explicit permission of the instructor and appropriate attribution. This includes, but is not limited to:
• Creating or revising drafts
• Editing your work
• Reviewing a peer's work
This excludes pre-existing software additions such as spelling and grammar checkers, which are acceptable.
Such use of AI text generators in this manner could be considered plagiarism and cheating according to Student Rule 20. More information may also be found at https://aggiehonor.tamu.edu or you may contact your instructor if you have questions.

chunks.title: Statement on Mental Health and Wellness
chunks.content: Texas A&M University recognizes that mental health and wellness are critical factors influencing a student's academic success and overall wellbeing. Students are encouraged to engage in healthy self-care practices by utilizing the resources and services available through University Health Services. The TELUS Health Student Support app provides access to professional counseling in multiple languages anytime, anywhere by phone or chat, and the 988 Suicide & Crisis Lifeline offers 24-hour emergency support at 988 or 988lifeline.org.
